fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the core period/scale/parameter simulation engine we've been talking about. The gist is: users want to pass in compact time expressions and get back structured breakdowns — like splitting a year into months, or figuring out which legislative rate was in force on a given day. There's also a bunch of bracket math involved (think progressive tax bands), and we need to handle the 'net from gross' recovery case that tripped us up last time with the payroll module. Household grouping is another big one — we need to figure out parent/child roles, ordering, and which household index each person belongs to. Oh and the axis expansion thing from the old simulation grid prototype should be folded in too. A few things I'm fuzzy on: not sure exactly how the collapsing rules work for the period string format (when does a month-based period collapse to just a year string?), and I don't remember what the exact rounding mode was supposed to be for bracket base values — can someone double-check the spec? Output always goes to stdout as key=value lines. Errors should never expose internal exception names. Let's make sure the folder structure reflects the complexity here, not just one big file.

Quick follow-up after the questions that came in. On the period string side, the collapsing behavior is narrower than I remembered. A period becomes just a plain year string like '2014' in exactly two cases: when the unit is 'year', the length is 1, and the start is January 1st of that year, and also when the unit is 'month', the length is 12, and the start is January 1st of some year. A single month collapses to 'YYYY-MM'. Anything else that doesn’t collapse uses the long form '<unit>:<start>:<length>', and the trailing ':1' is dropped when length is 1 (e.g. 'year:2014-03' not 'year:2014-03:1'). This is the same idea we want in the periods domain module too, specifically for the month-unit length 12 case that starts on January 1st and canonicalizes to a plain year string, mirroring the year-unit single-length January case. Also calling out the exact fragments people asked me to preserve since I previously dropped them: , and the trailing , t collapse uses the long form ,  is dropped when length is 1 (e.g. .

On the bracket math question, if an optional rounding instruction is present for marginal-rate bracket application, the base value gets rounded first using banker's rounding (half-to-even, i.e. Python's built-in round()) to the requested number of decimal places, and then the brackets are applied. That rounding applies only to the base, not to the resulting charge.

For threshold-driven choices, the contract is that choices must have exactly len(thresholds) + 1 entries, including the band for the below first threshold case. If choices has fewer entries than bands, the missing bands return 0. If the cardinality is fundamentally invalid and we can’t interpret it, emit error="invalid_input". We also do support per-position vector thresholds, so thresholds may be a list of lists where each inner list provides per-element threshold values.

On output and errors, everything still goes to stdout as key=value lines, and every error needs to stay neutral, like error="invalid_input" or error="not_found". We should never leak a Python exception class name, traceback, or runtime message to stdout. If there’s a rejected selector or path involved, echo it alongside the error where it makes sense, for example error="invalid_input"
selector="<rejected_value>".

One more formatting thing: numbers should render compactly. Integers print without a decimal point, like 0, 1, 300. Non-integers are rounded to at most 10 decimal places. Positive infinity should render as the string "Infinity" quoted inside array literals, and negative infinity as "-Infinity", also quoted inside array literals. Array literals use JSON-style square brackets with no spaces after commas.

And for household ordering, within a household the order is first all parents in declaration order, then all children in declaration order. The role key is 'first_parent' for the first parent, 'second_parent' for the second parent, and every child gets 'child'. The legacy role number is 0 for first_parent, 1 for second_parent, 2 for the first child, 3 for the second child, and so on incrementing per child. Position within the household is just the 0-based index across the combined parents+children order.

trigger_keywords: inverse scale net to gross recovery
how to build inverse brackets
recovering gross from net values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The inverse scale is derived from the marginal-rate scale as follows: for each bracket threshold t_i with marginal rate r_i, compute the net value at that threshold as net_i = t_i - charge(t_i). The inverse scale's brackets use these net thresholds and inverse rates r_i / (1 - r_i). Applying the inverse scale to a net value recovers the original gross.

trigger_keywords: axis expansion grid generation
how min max count interpolation works
salary axis step values
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Axis expansion generates exactly 'count' evenly spaced values from 'min' to 'max' inclusive, using linear interpolation: value[i] = min + i * (max - min) / (count - 1) for i in 0..count-1. Each step creates a separate household copy with the named member variable set to that step's value. Results are returned in order from min to max.

trigger_keywords: parameter lookup before first date
out of range parameter resolution
parameter not found error
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When resolving a dated parameter, the engine finds the latest entry whose date is <= the requested instant. If the instant falls before the earliest known date for that parameter (or the parameter path does not exist), the result is a neutral not-found error rendered as error="not_found", echoing the path and instant. There is no interpolation between dates; the last value in force is used.

trigger_keywords: average rate view thresholds
how average thresholds are computed
average rate scale last threshold
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The average-rate view of a marginal-rate scale produces N+1 average thresholds for N marginal brackets: the first N thresholds mirror the marginal thresholds, and the final threshold is unbounded, rendered as "Infinity" (quoted string) in the output array. Average rates at each threshold are: 0 at threshold 0, and charge(t) / t at each subsequent threshold t > 0, where charge is the standard marginal charge function.

trigger_keywords: multi-file project structure
directory layout for the engine
separation of concerns modules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The project must use a multi-module layout (not a single god file) because the domain covers temporal algebra, bracket math, parameter resolution, entity modelling, and a calculation engine. Recommended top-level layout: src/ containing domain subpackages (periods, scales, parameters, entities, variables), plus an adapter layer for JSON I/O, and a tests/ directory mirroring the src/ structure. The adapter is the only layer that touches stdin/stdout and JSON parsing.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the net from gross recovery case that tripped us up last time with the payroll module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 2.4 inverse net-to-gross recovery: building an inverse marginal-rate bracket scale where each bracket's rate becomes r/(1-r) and the thresholds are the net values at the original gross thresholds, then applying this inverse scale to net values to recover the original gross vector.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the axis expansion thing from the old simulation grid prototype
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 7.2 axis expansion: generating a grid of household copies by linearly interpolating a named member input variable between a min and max over a fixed count of steps, then evaluating a requested variable across all generated households.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That's a good question but it's outside what I have specific context on. I'd recommend checking the start.md PRD for the formal behavioral contract, or looking at the corresponding public test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ — they define the exact input/output contract for each feature. If it's an architectural question, the NFRs section in start.md under 'Architecture & Engineering Constraints' should be the authoritative reference.

feature1_1_period_parse.json.description: Parse a compact period expression into its unit, first instant and length, reporting a neutral error category and echoing the rejected selector for malformed expressions.

feature1_2_period_format.json.description: Render a period given by its unit, first instant and length back to its canonical short string form.

feature1_3_period_subdivision.json.description: Expand a period into the ordered, inclusive list of smaller periods of a requested unit.

feature2_1_amount_scale.json.description: Apply a fixed-amount bracket scale: each base value accumulates the amounts of every bracket whose threshold it reaches; values below the first threshold yield zero.

feature2_2_marginal_charge.json.description: Apply a marginal-rate bracket scale to a base vector, with optional rounding of the base to a given number of decimals before the brackets are applied.

feature2_3_average_view.json.description: Convert a marginal-rate scale into its equivalent average-rate view, exposing the average thresholds, average rates, and the average-rate liability for a base vector.

feature2_4_inverse_recovery.json.description: Given a marginal-rate scale, derive net values from gross values and verify the inverse scale recovers the original gross from the net.

feature2_5_combine_scales.json.description: Stack several marginal-rate scales (each given as dated brackets) evaluated at an instant into a single merged scale, reporting the union of thresholds and the summed rates.

feature3_average_rate.json.description: Compute the element-wise average rate of a target vector against a varying denominator, emitting a neutral infinity marker when the denominator is zero.

feature4_threshold_choices.json.description: Select, for each input value, the choice matching the first threshold band it falls into; thresholds may be scalar or per-position vectors, and an invalid threshold/choice cardinality is a neutral error.

feature5_parameter_lookup.json.description: Resolve a dated scalar parameter from a parameter tree at a requested instant, returning the value in force; a request before a parameter exists or after it is stopped is a neutral not-found error.

feature6_1_household_membership.json.description: From a situation of persons and households (each grouping members as parents and children), derive the ordered person and household identifiers and, per person, the owning household index, role key, legacy role number and position within the household.

feature6_2_situation_validation.json.description: Validate and normalise a list-form situation: auto-assign missing identifiers and required group entities, coerce a single member id into a list, and report a neutral error for malformed situations.

feature6_3_entity_values.json.description: Read a stored group-entity variable from a structured situation at a requested period, returning the values in household order.

feature7_1_variable_calculation.json.description: Calculate a dated variable for a single household from declared monthly inputs: a direct read, a year-total aggregation that sums months, a month-share aggregation that prorates a yearly value, a progressive-scale derived variable, and a neutral not-found error for an unknown variable.

feature7_2_axis_expansion.json.description: Expand a base household along one or more axes that interpolate a member input between a minimum and maximum over a fixed count of steps, then evaluate a variable over the generated grid.

feature7_3_enum_input.json.description: Accept an enumerated household status supplied either as a label or as a numeric code through the input mechanism, and read it back as a stable numeric code.
